title: Round willow basket (small)
subtitle: A traditional round willow basket in three-rod waling and randing, 25 cm diameter
excerpt: A small round willow basket is the first complete basket project: it covers every fundamental technique in order. The base is staked and woven, the sides waled upright, randed to height, and bordered down. A 25 cm diameter basket in buff willow can be completed in one session of four to five hours once the preparation is understood.
sourceNotes: Round willow basket construction documented in British basketmaking traditions; Crofton Sibbett's Basket Making (1888) and Museum of English Rural Life basketry records.

glossaryTerms.definition: Single-rod weaving in which one willow rod weaves in and out of the stakes in a simple over-one-under-one pattern. The main side-weaving technique for the basket body.

A three-rod weave worked simultaneously, with each rod passing in front of two stakes and behind one. Produces a strong, ribbed band used at the base upsett and at the border to lock the weaving in place.
The first waling band worked at the base of the basket to turn the stakes upright from the base position into the side-weaving position.
A blunt-tipped pointed tool used to open the weave to insert new rods and to tuck border ends into the basket structure.
The upright structural rod around which weavers are woven. On a round basket, stakes radiate from the base and define the circumference.

projectSchedule.title: Weave the basket in one session
projectSchedule.body: Base, upsett, randing, and border. Four to five hours. Soak willows the day before.

projectSchedule.title: Basket dried and set
projectSchedule.body: After two to three days of drying, the basket is rigid and the border is fully set.

content.text: A small round basket teaches every fundamental basketmaking skill in sequence: weaving a base, turning the
, and working a border to finish. The same techniques apply to any round basket at any scale; once learned at this size, moving to larger baskets is a matter of proportion rather than new technique.

attrs.title: Prepare your willows the day before
attrs.body: Buff, white, or brown willow rods need soaking before use to restore flexibility. Brown (unstripped) willow: soak overnight in cold water. Buff (stripped and boiled) willow: soak for one hour. White (stripped, unboiled) willow: soak for twenty to thirty minutes, then mellow in a damp cloth for several hours. Never use dry willow; it will crack rather than bend at the border.

content.text: Buff willow in two lengths: base sticks from 3 to 4 ft rods (thick butt end), stakes from 3 ft rods (medium), and weavers from 2 to 2.5 ft rods (fine tips). For a 25 cm round basket you will need approximately: 8 base sticks, 16 stakes, and 20 to 30 weavers. Purchase from a basketry supplier; willow lengths are sold by the bundle in feet.
for opening the weave and tucking ends. A rapping iron for tamping weavers down to produce an even side surface. No other tools are needed.

Cut 8 base sticks to 28 cm. Pierce 4 of them through the centre with the bodkin and thread the other 4 through the slit so you have a cross of 4 sticks by 4 sticks. Tie this slath together with 2 rounds of pairing weave (two weavers alternating). After two rounds, open the sticks out to radiate evenly and continue pairing until the base is 20 to 22 cm in diameter. The base is woven flat on the lap.

Cut 16 stakes to 60 cm, point one end with the secateurs, and insert one on each side of each base stick by pushing the point into the base weave beside the stick. Fold each stake upright against the base and tie them together at the top to hold them vertical. Work a 3-rod
of three rounds to lock the stakes upright and establish the base shape of the basket.

to weave the sides to about 12 cm height. On a 16-stake round basket with an even number of stakes, randing requires that the stakes be an odd number for the pattern to work without a join; use 15 or 17 stakes if a proper rand is desired, or use a different starting order. Consult the randing technique entry for the correct approach. Tamp each row down with the rapping iron to keep the weave tight.
Work two to three rounds of three-rod waling at the top of the side weaving to lock the rand in place and provide a firm base for the border.

The trac border is the simplest finish: take each stake in turn, bend it down alongside the next stake on the right, pass behind the following stake, and tuck the tip into the weave with the bodkin. Work all the way round. The border locks all stake ends into the structure and produces the neat rolled top edge of the basket.
Trim all protruding ends with the secateurs, cutting close to the structure. Leave the basket in a warm dry room for two to three days to set and dry. No finish is applied to willow basketry.
Work a three-rod wale border rather than a trac border for a thicker, more decorative top edge.
Add twisted willow handles after the border is complete: twist two rods together and loop them through the top waling band on opposite sides of the basket.
Scale up to a 35 cm diameter basket using the same technique: use longer stakes and more weavers; the process is identical.

Keep out of prolonged wet or damp conditions; the basket will soften if saturated repeatedly. Dry naturally after any exposure to moisture. A willow basket in regular use will last many years with no maintenance.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems encountered on a first basket.

items.symptom: The base becomes dished rather than flat during weaving
items.cause: The base sticks are bowing downward because the weave tension is inconsistent, or the base was not woven on a flat surface.
items.fix: Weave the base in the lap with downward pressure on the sticks at each pass. If the base has dished, dampen it and press flat under a heavy book overnight.

items.symptom: The stakes splay out too wide or pull in too tight as the sides grow
items.cause: Weaving tension is inconsistent; the splay is set by the angle at which rods are woven in.
items.fix: After each two or three rounds of randing, step back and check the basket profile from the side. Adjust the angle of the weavers in the next round to correct the flare.

items.symptom: Rods crack or break at the bend in the border
items.cause: The willow was not adequately soaked or mellowed before use.
items.fix: Dampen the border area with a wet cloth and allow to absorb for ten minutes before continuing. For future baskets, soak longer and mellow in a damp cloth for at least an hour after soaking.
